qas.q: Steps in the Risk Management Process
qas.a:
1. Understand the business
2. Identify Assets
3. Identify risks / vulnerabilities / threats
4. Impact Analysis
5. Prioritize Risks
6. Evaluate Controls
7. Risk treatment / Apply controls

âœ”This process is subjective
âœ”Is used to make security policy decisions.
âœ”Needs to consider the ENTIRE IT Environment

Defines how the organization will protect its assets and how it will respond to threats and incidents. Security should be separate from IT to stay objective.
It includes:
âœ”Roles and responsibilities
âœ”Risk Management
âœ”Security Processes
âœ”Acceptable use

It is VERY General - few specifics
A meeting used to discuss the scope of the audit

Risk = Probability * Impact
or
Risk = Asset Value Vulnerability Threat

âœ”All audit plans, programs, activities, tests, findings, and incidents
âœ”The bridge between the Audit Objectives and and the Final Report
Whatever we are trying to protect against

Weakness or gap in our protection efforts.
Absence of proper security measures

âœ”Inherent Risk - The Business with no controls
âœ”Residual Risk - Risk that remains after controls
âœ”Detection Risk - Risk that Auditors fail to detect a misstatement in the log
âœ”Control Risk - Risk that the controls won't catch the problem
âœ”Audit Risk = Inherent Risk * Control Risk * Detection Risk

Risk Mitigation / Risk Reduction - An umbrella (Add Controls)
Risk Avoidance - don't go out
Risk Acceptance - just get wet
Risk Transfer - insurance or Vendor
Is responsible for managing Risk
Are derived as a result of a risk assessment

A document that defines the Internal IS audit function's responsibility; authority, roles, scope, objectives, and accountability.
âœ”Approved and Changed by The Audit Committee
âœ”Independent from IS and IT
âœ”It is a Static Document
âœ”Does NOT include audit calendar, audit planning, yearly resource allocation, travel expenses, and other routine audit activities.

âœ”Verifies processes
âœ”Checks for controls
âœ”Attribute Sampling is used (the control is there or it is not)
âœ”Is done before Substantive Testing
âœ”Usually checks a Procedure or Policy

âœ”Verifies data or transactions of the data
âœ”Checks for completeness, accuracy, and validity of the data
âœ”Variable Sampling is used
âœ”Is done after Compliance Testing

Compliance Testing verifies Processes with Attribute Sampling
Substantive Testing verifies Transactions with Variable Sampling

âœ”A self-regulation activity
âœ”Done during the preliminary survey phase
âœ”Facilitated by the Internal Auditor
âœ”Success dependent Involvement of Line Management in control monitoring
âœ”A technique that allows managers and work teams directly involved in business units, functions or processes to participate in assessing the organization's risk management and control processes

âœ”To leverage the Internal Audit Function by shifting some of the control monitoring responsibilities to the functional areas
âœ”To concentrate on areas of High Risk
âœ”To provide early identification of risk
âœ”To enhance audit responsibilities (not replace)

âœ”Early Detection of Risk
âœ”More effective and improved controls
âœ”Assurance provided to stakeholders and customers.

âœ”It is Objective
âœ”Non-Judgmental
âœ”Probability of error can be objectively quantified
âœ”Each item has an equal chance of selection
âœ”Minimizes Detection Risk
âœ”Can be used to draw a conclusion on the entire population

âœ”It is Subjective
âœ”Also known as Judgmental Sampling
âœ”Cannot be objectively quantified
âœ”Sampling depends on the judgment of the auditor

âœ”Used in Compliance Testing
âœ”Some number of items in the sample comply, some do not
âœ”Expressed in Percentage
âœ”Answers "how Many?" (Not "How Much?" like Variable Sampling)
âœ”"This many failed to get approval

âœ”You are Sampling a specific Variable
âœ”Used in Substantive Testing
âœ”Allows us to understand "how much" (not how many like Attribute Sampling)
âœ”Expressed in units like weight, money, or other values
âœ”"It cost this much on average"
âœ”Separation of the target population into different groups, called strata, and the selection of samples from each stratum

âœ”Used when very few errors are expected to be found
âœ”Stopped once an error is found

âœ”Used to find Fraud
âœ”If Fraud is found, report it to the authorities

âœ”A higher number will result from the use of a larger sample size.
âœ”The use of a larger sample size will result in a high CCE
âœ”If controls are strong, the CCE / Sample Size may be lowered by the auditor
âœ”Meaning, if there are lots of controls you trust, you can use a smaller sample size.

âœ”To Identify the effectiveness of existing controls
âœ”To Identify process lapses and areas of improvement
âœ”To Identify exceptions and deviations
âœ”To identify areas of fraud
âœ”To evaluate data quality and identify areas with poor data quality
âœ”To assess risk and to plan audit activities
âœ”Can be effective for an IS auditor in both the planning and fieldwork phases of the audit

1. Determine the objectives and scope of analytics
2. Requirement gathering and obtaining the data
3. Determine the sufficiency and reliability of the data
4. Execute the test by running scripts / performing analytical tests.
5. Results/conclusions of the tests to be documented
6. Review of results/conclusion by a qualified person
7. Retain the results such as scripts, files, macro programs and data files.

âœ”Helps the auditor to capture and analyze the data during an audit
âœ”Good for complex environments or processes
âœ”Insures independence of auditors while capturing relevant data
âœ”Provides reliability of the source of the data and therefore reassurance on audit findings (Very Important)
âœ”Some are General Audit Software (GAS)
âœ” Help in effective and efficient detection of exceptions or irregularities

qas.q: CAATs - What to watch out for
qas.a:
âœ”Ensure data integrity by safeguarding Confidentiality, Integrity, and Authenticity (Most important)
âœ”Get approval to install it by the auditee
âœ”Only use Read-Only rights while accessing data

âœ”Real Time or Near Real Time auditing.
âœ”Quick results

âœ”Like AV or IDS
âœ”In general Continuous Auditing happens before Continuous Monitoring
Should be independent of Continuous Auditing
âœ”Usually handed over to process owners
Continuous Auditing + Continuous Monitoring

âœ”The transmission of transactions (information) between two organizations
âœ”Promotes more efficient paperless environment
âœ”Consists of Transmission, Translation, and Storage of transaction. It is initiated or destined for application processing
âœ”Auditors should trace the transaction from beginning to end

1. Communications Handler - Involves the process for transmitting and receiving electronic documents between trading partners via dedicated channels.
2. Interface - This is the interface between the Application System and the Communication Handler. It has two parts:
âœ”Translation - translates data into trading partners format
âœ”Application Interface - It moves electronic transactions to or from the application systems.
3. Application System - It processes data sent to or received from the trading partner.

âœ”Transaction Authorizations don't really occur in an automated system
âœ”No inherent authorization occurs (Greatest Risk)
âœ”Needs a Trading Partner Agreement to cover specific legal liability (MOST IMPORTANT)
âœ”Performance issues could affect both parties
âœ”Unauthorized access, data integrity, confidentiality, loss or duplicate transactions

âœ”Ensure the integrity of message format and content to avoid transmission errors.
âœ”Ensure control at receiving end
âœ”Logs to be maintained
âœ”Ensure messages are properly authorized
âœ”Direct channels are best to prevent wiretapping
âœ”Use encryption
âœ”Use batch totals
Ensures CIA - Confidentiality, Integrity, and Authenticity and Non-Repudiation of transactions
To assess transaction reasonableness and validity

âœ”Use control fields withing an EDI message
âœ”Use VAN sequential control numbers or reports
âœ”Use acknowledgment transaction to sender
âœ”Use segmentation of duties for high-risk transactions
Log it all

âœ”Log each transaction on Receipt
âœ”Build segment count totals into transaction set trailer by the sender
âœ”Use check digit to detect transposition and transcription error

Includes:
âœ”Operational Audit
âœ”IS Audit
âœ”Financial Audit

1. Identify risks
2. Identify key controls
3. Understand the design of the key controls
4. Test to see if key controls are supported by the IT system
5. Test to see if controls are effective
6. Issue combined report on risks, controls, and weaknesses

âœ”Easy to link controls and audit procedures
âœ”Helps with allocating and utilization of IT resources
âœ”Help link good corporate governance and reliable financial statements (MOST IMPORTANT)

âœ”Closure meeting ensures that there have been no misunderstandings or misrepresentation of facts.
âœ”Closing meeting helps to enhance the understanding between the auditor and the the auditee in terms of what was presented, discussed, and agreed upon.
âœ”For communication of audit results, IS auditor is ultimately responsible to senior management and the audit committee of the board of directors. If access is not granted for such a discussion, then it would limit the independence of the audit function
âœ”During the assignment, control weaknesses observed which are not in the scope of the audit, should still be reported to management.
âœ”ISACA's IS Audit and Assurance Standards on reporting requires that the IS auditor has sufficient and appropriate evidence to support the reported results
âœ”Generally accepted audit practice requires reporting of findings even if corrective action has already occurred
âœ”In some instances, IS auditor may be requested from audit management to assist in implementing recommendations. IS auditor should explain that is a role conflict and would affect independence.
âœ”Findings are clearly tracked back to evidence (Most IMPORTANT)

âœ”Auditor should elaborate on the significance of the finding and the risk of not correcting it.
âœ”The goal is to enlighten the Auditee
The auditor should use professional judgement to ensure that a sufficient amount of this will be collected

âœ”It is advisable to conduct confirmatory audit after the timelines agreed by management for remediation action. As a generally accepted practice, auditor should not dictate timelines
âœ”Primary purpose of conducting follow-up audits is to validate remediation action

âœ”In-sourced/In-house - Activity performed by the organization's staff
âœ”Outsource - Activity performed by Vendor's staff
âœ”Hybrid - Activity performed both
____________________________________________________________________________
âœ”Onsite - Staff works onsite in IT department
âœ”Offsite/Nearshore - Staff works in a remote location in the same geographical location
âœ”Offshore - Staff works in different geographical location

qas.q: Functions should NOT be outsourced if:
qas.a:
âœ”It is a core function of the organization
âœ”It is a function that requires specific knowledge, processes, and critical staffs that cannot be replicated externally or on another location
âœ”In case of contract or regulatory restrictions

qas.q: Functions that CAN be outsourced if:
qas.a:
âœ”If can be performed with the same or higher quality or the same or lower price
âœ”Organization has sufficient experience managing 3rd parties.

âœ”Expert service can be obtained from outside so organization can concentrate on its core business (this is PRIMARY reason)
âœ”Cost Savings

1. Define the Function
2. Define the Service Level Requirements
3. Know the in-house cost
4. Conduct due diligence of service providers
5. Confirm contractual or regulatory requirements
6. Get the contract! MOST important

âœ” SLA needs to contain measurable performance requirements
âœ”Escrow arrangement for proprietary software
âœ”Use multiple suppliers to reduce risk of dependency
âœ”Periodic Performance Reviews (MOST important)
âœ”Establish cross-functional contract management team
âœ”Establish necessary controls for foreseen contingencies
âœ”Get references from other sources
âœ”Make sure you have Detailed and Correctly Applied Specifications (BEST)
Accountability. Final accountability lies with the organization.

âœ”Service Level Agreement to contain measurable performance requirements (Do this FIRST)
âœ”Confidentiality agreements protecting both parties
âœ”"Right to Audit" Clause
âœ”Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery Provisions
âœ”Protection of Intellectual Property Rights
âœ”Requirements for CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability) and privacy too.
âœ”Gain-sharing performance bonuses (Help the MOST to improve service and minimize costs)

qas.q: Role of IS Auditor - Monitoring Outsourced Activities
qas.a:
âœ”Regular reviews of contract and service levels
âœ”Review of Outsourcer's documented procedures and outcome of their quality programs
âœ”Regular audits to certify that the process and procedures meet the quality standards
âœ”If Off-shored, Legal Jurisdiction is the most important issue
Relates to the long-term direction an enterprise wants to take in leveraging IT for improving its business processes

âœ”Requires setting up IT function at remote or offshore location
âœ”It may or may not involve outsourcing
âœ”Many organizations globalize their IT function for the same reasons cited for outsourcing
âœ”The following issues need to be addressed for smooth functioning of IT function from the offsite location:
âœ”Legal and Regulatory issues
âœ”Continuity of Operations
âœ”Telecommunication issues
âœ”Cross border and cross cultural issues

âœ”Shows Direction for IT

IT should support business and align as per business objectives
âœ”Close alignment is evident when there is a clear mapping, linking, or cascading of IT strategy to business strategy
âœ”Business processes and objectives should always be the driver for IT requirements.
âœ”When formulating IT Strategy, the prime consideration should be Business Strategy
âœ”The 1st step in reviewing an organization's IT strategy is to review/understand the business plan
âœ”IS, to be effective, should be in line with enterprise requirements. Hence Enterprise Requirements should form the basis of Security Requirements
âœ”To govern IT effectively, IT and Business objectives can best be assured by the involvement of top management.
âœ”When formulating IT Strategy, the Enterprise must consider:
ðŸŽˆBusiness Objectives
ðŸŽˆRisks and benefits they can bring to the business

Is used to establish, monitor, and evaluate (optimize) IT performance in terms of:

âœ”Future Orientation (Is IT prepared?)
âœ”Operational Excellence (is IT Efficient?)
âœ”User Orientation (Are users satisfied with IT?)

âœ”It is the most effective means to aid the IT strategy committee and management in achieving IT governance through proper IT and business alignment

âœ”Adds value to strategic IT decision making

Need to be defined BEFORE BSC implementation

âœ”Internal Processes
âœ”Ability to Innovate

CIA

âœ”The manager in charge of the business function
âœ”Owner of the data and the system under development

An IS auditor should first evaluate the definition of the minimum baseline level by ensuring the sufficiency of the control baseline to meet security requirements.

âœ”The cutoff point in the design; also referred to as Design Freeze
âœ”Prevents Scope Creep
Are accountable for protection of their resources

âœ”Organizations NEED to have a list of applicable laws and regulations

âœ”Is a process to provide adequate confidence that an item or product conforms to established norms

âœ”Prevents defects

âœ”Is the process for conducting tests or reviews to verify a product is defect-free and fulfills the requirements of the user.

âœ”Reactive
âœ”Finds Defects

âœ”Data Analytic Tool.

qas.q: System Control Audit Review File/Embedded Audit Module - SCARF/EAM
qas.a:
âœ”Type of Continuous Auditing
âœ”An embedded / inbuilt software module is used to continuously monitor transactions as they are processed
âœ”This method is used to collect data for special audit purposes.
âœ”Records only those transactions which are of special audit significance such as above a specified limit or related to a standard deviation.
âœ”A printout / output is produced regularly to be examined
âœ”Used when regular processing cannot be interrupted
Identifies specific program logic that has not been tested, and analyzes programs statements have been executed

âœ”Type of Continuous Auditing
âœ”Pictures (Snaps) are taken of the transactions as they move through various stages in the application system
âœ”Transaction snaps are taken pre- and post-processing.
âœ”Used when Audit Trail is required
âœ”Three important considerations:
ðŸŽˆLocation where the snaps are taken
ðŸŽˆTime of snaps
ðŸŽˆReporting of snaps

âœ”A type of Continuous Auditing
âœ”This is a variation of the SCARF method
âœ”Can be used with DBMS (databases)
âœ”The database sends criteria based copies of transactions to the CIS, which replicates the processing the transaction gets in the actual application
âœ”The results from the application are compared to the results from CIS.
âœ”Best for pre-defined highly complex criteria

âœ”Type of Continuous Auditing
âœ”Audit Software that captures suspicious transactions
âœ”Criteria for suspicious transactions are designed by the auditors per requirement
âœ”Useful when Early Detection is needed

âœ”Unit - Single Module
âœ”Integrated or Interface - Two or more Modules
âœ”System - Usually in non-production
âœ”Final Acceptance Testing

âœ”Done by Developer on a particular module
âœ”White Box testing approach
âœ”Testing of two or more modules that pass information to each other

âœ”To evaluate the entire system's functionality.
âœ”Includes these types of testing:
ðŸŽˆRecovery - ability to recover after hw / sw failure
ðŸŽˆSecurity - System includes access controls and no new security holes
ðŸŽˆLoad - performance with lots of data
ðŸŽˆVolume - uses increasing loads to find max volume
ðŸŽˆStress - uses increasing numbers of users to find max users
ðŸŽˆPerformance - compare to other systems using well-defined benchmarks
Done to optimize performance

âœ”Performed after system staff is satisfied with the system tests
âœ”QAT - Quality Assurance Testing
âœ”UAT - User Acceptance Testing

âœ”Ensures changes in a program have not introduced new errors
âœ”Data should be the same used for previous tests
âœ”Make sure the new or modified system can work in the specified environment without adversely impacting existing system
âœ”Testing system in one location before implementing it at other locations

âœ”Compares results of processing on the old and the new system
âœ”Purpose is to ensure implementation of new system meets the user requirements
âœ”Abilty to roll back is easy

âœ”Program Logic is Tested
âœ”Applicable for Unit Testing
âœ”Detailed knowledge of the program is needed

âœ”Functionality is tested, not program logic
âœ”Applicable for UAT (User Acceptance Testing)
âœ”Does not need detailed knowledge of the program

Alpha:
âœ”Done by internal user
âœ”Done before Beta testing
âœ”May not involve full functionality testing

Beta:
âœ”Done by External user
âœ”Done after Alpha testing
âœ”Generally involves full functionality testing

Bottom-Up Approach:
âœ”Begin testing of individual units/programs/modules and work upward until a complete system is tested
âœ”Advantages:
ðŸŽˆTests can be started before all programs are complete
ðŸŽˆErrors in critical modules can be found early

Top-Down Approach:
âœ”Tests start from a broader level and work down towards individual until/programs/modules
âœ”More appropriate for Prototype development
ðŸŽˆInterface errors can be detected earlier
ðŸŽˆConfidence in the system can be achieved earlier

Unit Testing - Tests individual program or module

Interface/Integrated Testing - Tests connections between two or more components that pass information

qas.q: Regression vs Sociability vs Integration Testing
qas.a:
Regression Testing - changes have not introduced new errors

Sociability Testing - changes still work in the existing system
Interface Testing

Integration Testing - to ensure data flows between modules
Seems to be the same thing to me

âœ”A mathematically calculated value that is added to data to ensure the original data has not been altered
âœ”Used to ensure accuracy
âœ”Identifies Transcription and Transposition errors
Think - Transpose Digits when Transcribing

âœ”An extra bit is added to the data. The bit simply says whether the number of 1 bits is even or odd.
âœ”It has a 50% of detecting an error
âœ”Used for transmission errors, integrity, and completeness (Parity, Checksum, and CRC)

âœ”Same as Parity but much more complex
âœ”Used for transmission errors, integrity, and completeness (Parity, Checksum, and CRC)

qas.q: CRC - Cyclic Redundancy Checksum / Redundancy Checksum
qas.a:
âœ”More advanced Checksum
âœ”Used for transmission errors, integrity, and completeness (Parity, Checksum, and CRC)

âœ”Check Digit - Transcription or Transposition

âœ”Transmission Errors, Completeness, or Integrity, the following are true:
ðŸŽˆParity - Weak
ðŸŽˆChecksum - Medium
ðŸŽˆCRC - Strong
CRC with the ability to correct the error

âœ”A set of rules that the database uses to ensure that the data between related tables is valid.
âœ”Uses Foreign Keys in linked tables
To sign an individual executable/interpreted code digitally so that users have confidence the code they run is the actual code from the developer.
Identify erroneous, unusual or invalid transactions (preventative)

âœ”Is a design or optimization process for a relational database that increases redundancy
Industry standard that facilitates portability
Rules in a Database that help ensure the quality of information
Records or Rows in a Relational Database

qas.a: The best way to secure a web server

âœ”Technique for estimating project duration

The time that an activity can be delayed without delaying the entire project.

ðŸŽˆOptimistic / Best

âœ”Estimates software size (and how long it will take to code)

âœ”A software size estimator.

âœ”What have you completed so far?

qas.q: Information Systems Development Project Cost Estimation
qas.a:
âœ”Analogous Estimating - By using estimates of prior projects
âœ”Parametric Estimating - Take Analogous and and add statistical data like estimated employee hours materials costs, etc.
âœ”Bottom-Up Estimating - Estimated the cost of each activity in greatest detail - Most Accurate, Most Time Consuming
âœ”Actual Costs
âœ”Software Size Estimating - By complexity and lines of code

âœ”Advantage is that it prevents project cost overruns and delays from the scheduled delivery

âœ”Gantt -- Monitors progress and milestones
âœ”EVA -- What is done so far, What is left?
âœ”FPA & SLOC --Estimate software size and complexity (FPA is Better)

âœ”Allows the programmer to just start writing a program without spending much time on pre-planning documentation

qas.q: SDLC Phase 1 - Planning and Feasibility Study
qas.a:

âœ” A certificate binds a digital signature to an entity, whereas a digital signature is to ensure that a data/information remain secure from the point it was issued.
âœ”Digital certificates are used to verify the trustworthiness of a person (sender), while digital signatures are used to verify the trustworthiness of the data being sent.
âœ”Digital Signatures do not ensure confidentiality
âœ”Provides sender authenticity, message integrity, and non-repudiation
Provides Authentication of the Web Site to be surfed

âœ”A process where digital code is attached to an electronically transmitted document to verify it content and sender's identity
âœ”Ensures Integrity, Authentication, and Non-repudiation
âœ”Does NOT ensure Confidentiality

1. Create Hash Value of the Message
2. Encrypt the Hash of the Message with the Private Key of the sender /signer
3. Receiver creates a Hash of the Message
4. Receiver decrypts the Hash with Senders public key
5. Receiver compares the two Hashes
A mathematical algorithm which gives unique fixed string for any given message

âœ”Encrypt the Hash with the Sender's Private Key

âœ”Sender Hashes the message and encrypts the hash with Sender's Private Key.

âœ”Sender Hashes the message and encrypts the hash with Sender's Private Key.

âœ”Does not provide encryption, only Authentication and Integrity

1. Applicant applies for Digital Certificate from Certification Authority (CA)
2. Certification Authority (CA) delegates the verification process to the Registration Authority (RA)
3. Registration Authority (RA) validate information and tells Certification Authority (CA) to issue the certificate
4. Certification Authority (CA) issues the Certificate and manages the Certificate
5. Certification Authority (CA) maintains a list of certificates that have been revoked/terminated before its expiration date. This list is know as the Certificate Revocation List (CRL)
6. The Certification Authority (CA) will also have Certification Practice Statement (CPS) in which standard operation procedures (SOPs) for issuance of certificates and other relevant details are documented.
A list, maintained by the Certification Authority (CA), of certificates that have been compromised or revoked by the owner.

âœ”Validates and authenticates the holder of the certificate after issuance of the certificate

Private Cloud
Public Cloud
Hybrid Cloud
Community Cloud

The cloud is used exclusively for the benefit of a particular organization. It resides within the boundaries of the organization
âœ”More secure environment and less chance of data leakage
âœ”Centralized control of cloud by organization itself
âœ”SLA does not exist or is very weak

Open for use to the general public. Offered on the basis of pay per use basis
âœ”Highly Available
âœ”Highly Scalable
âœ”Affordable Cost
âœ”Less secure than other models
âœ”Strict SLAs

Combination of public and private. Initially, the private cloud is used, then additional resources in the public cloud are used.
âœ”More complex since more than 1 model is used
âœ”Less secure than private cloud
âœ”Highly Scaleable
âœ”Better SLA than private cloud

Cloud is used by specific community of consumers that have shared interests
âœ”Collaborative maintenance is required.
âœ”No single company has control over the cloud
âœ”Less secure than private cloud / more secure than public cloud
âœ”Cost effective

âœ”Verify whether regulations of the locations of infrastructure is aligned with enterprise requirement's
âœ”Contract to include terms to restrict the movement of assets within approved locations (MOST Important)
âœ”To prevent disclosure, encrypt the asset prior to migration to the Cloud Service Provider (CSP)
âœ”An Indemnity Clause included in the contract with the service provider

âœ”Verify the Cloud Service Provider's (CSP'S) physical security policy and ensure that it aligns with the enterprise's security policy
âœ”Obtain copy of independent security reviews or audit reports (BEST)
âœ”Bind the CSP through a contract to align with the enterprise's security policy and to implement necessary controls to ensure it
âœ”Verify CSP's disaster recovery plans and ensure that they contain the necessary arrangement to protect assets.

âœ”Verify CSP's technical specifications and controls that ensure that data are properly wiped off as per requirement
âœ”Contract should specify that upon contract expiration a mandatory data wipe carried out in the presence of a representative of the company

âœ”Contract should specify requirement for proper disposal of applications including objects, source and backups.
âœ”Contract should also include the non-compete clause

âœ”Contract should include "right to audit"
âœ”Contract should specify implementation of necessary controls to ensure access to only authorized users
âœ”Obtain copy of independent security reviews or audit reports of CSP

âœ”Control should specify requirement of notification to enterprise in case of any event.
âœ”Contract should specify availability of contracted capacity and same should not be directed to other tenants (Instance) without approval
âœ”To use a private cloud deployment (no Multi Tenancy)

âœ”To ensure availability of information systems and data on continuous basis
âœ”To ensure the integrity and confidentiality information and sensitive data while stored and in transit
âœ”To ensure compliance to relevant laws, regulations and standards

âœ”Clarity with respect to data ownership, data custody, and security administration related to the cloud environment
âœ”To consider legal requirements, laws, regulations and unique risk in the cloud environment (Legal is MOST important)
âœ”Limitation to "right to audit" clause as it may not be possible to audit physical perimeters of cloud environment

âœ”Always have vendors include this in the contracts
âœ”At minimum, you need to have access to 3rd party versions

âœ”Allows users to run multiple operating systems simultaneously on a single server
âœ”Main goal is to manage workloads by transforming traditional computing to make it more scaleable
âœ”Uses a physical machine's full capacity by distributing its capabilities among many users or environments
âœ”Provides an enterprise with a significant opportunity to increase efficiency and decrease costs
âœ”Creates a layer between the hardware and the guest OSs to manage shared memory and shared processing resources on the host

âœ”Server / Hardware
âœ”Hypervisor - Know as the HOST - software firmware or hardware that creates and runs the virtual machine environment
âœ”Guest machines: Virtual elements - servers, firewalls, or anything that was made

âœ”Bare Metal / Native Virtualization - Hypervisor runs directly on hardware without a host
âœ”Hosted Virtualization - Hypervisor runs on top of the Host's OS
âœ”Containerization - Containers run as an isolated process in userspace on the Host OS

âœ”Poor configuration of the host may create vulnerabilities for host as well as guest
âœ”Any attack against the host could affect all the guests
âœ”Inadequate security of management console can have risk of unapproved administrative access to the host's guests
âœ”Performance issues of the host's own OS could impact each of the host's guests
âœ”Risk of data leakage between guests if there is poor control for memory release and allocation

âœ”Installation of toolkits as a hypervisor below the Operating System and thus risk of interception of the Guest's OS.
âœ”Risk of improper configuration of the hypervisor partitioning resources (CPU, Memory, Drive Space, etc) can allow unauthorized access to resources
âœ”On hosted virtualization, mechanisms called guest tools can allow an attacker to gain access to particular resources
âœ”On hosted virtualization, products rarely have hypervisor access controls. Anyone who can launch as application on the host OS can run the hypervisor

âœ”Secure configurations and harden the hypervisors and guest images
âœ”Encrypt hypervisor management communications
âœ”Ensure regular patch updates for the hypervisor
âœ”Synchronize to time server
âœ”Disconnect all unused hardware
âœ”Disable Hypervisor services such as Clipboard and Filesharing between Guest OS and Host OS
âœ”Log and monitor security events of each OS
âœ”File integrity monitoring of the hypervisor
Policies, Procedures, mechanisms, systems, and other measures designed to reduce risk.

âœ”Physical - video surveillance (BEST Physical), fences
âœ”Technical/Logical - Encryption, computer access controls, logs
âœ”Administrative/Managerial - policies and procedures

âœ”Preventative - Login screen, Encryption
âœ”Detective - The VIEWING of Video and logs
âœ”Deterrent - Dogs, guards, visible video
âœ”Corrective - the act of improving a process found to be defective
âœ”Compensating - implemented when a direct control cannot be used
âœ”Recovery - Virus removal tool
Checked by the auditor conducting the process again (re-performance)
Checked by conducting a walk-through
Centralized systems that monitor and control entire sites.
âœ”Disseminate security alerts, guidelines, and updates to users and assist them in understanding the risks

âœ”Vulnerable computer that is set up to entice an intruder to break into it
âœ”Provides the MOST relevant information for proactively strengthening security settings
Severs shouldn't be over or under utilized.

âœ”With Best / Most Concerning / Worst questions, read them as though all the answers have all occurred, and you have to choose at this point.
âœ”Seems whenever they ask about Fraud, Integrity is the Answer
âœ”Auditor should audit high-risk things first, then work down. Work it from the Risk Assessment
âœ”When it comes to BCP, testing is usually the most important thing to do
âœ”DRP - always make sure you have enough resources in the other location.
âœ”Patching needs change management. (for testing and risk review)
âœ”Unencrypted passwords and data is a bigger issue than most other security issues
âœ”Preventative Controls are better than Detective Controls
âœ”From a Risk standpoint, loss of data is much worse than loss of function
âœ”"Gap" is tied to "Existing"
âœ”"Accountability" is tied to "Log/Audit Trail"
